title: Tenant provisioning DDL creates only 2 of ~24 module tables — new tenants are structurally broken
exploitability: Not an attack — triggered by normal signup. Every new tenant hits it.
description: generateTenantDDL() emits CREATE TABLE for only `departments` and `employees`. The tenant schema module defines ~24 tables (payroll_runs, payslips, wage_files, expenses, leave_types/requests/balances, attendance shifts/records/exceptions, documents, compliance_checks, final_settlements, notifications, audit_logs, recruitment, retention, ai, qiwa_contracts, policy_documents, guide_maps, employee_invitations, employment_history). None of the other tables are created when a tenant is provisioned at runtime.
expectedBehavior: createTenantSchema() should materialize the complete, current tenant table set (every table exported from schema/tenant/index.ts) with all constraints, indexes and triggers, so all modules work for a freshly signed-up tenant.
actualBehavior: Only departments + employees are created. Payroll, leave, attendance, expenses, recruitment, audit, compliance etc. tables do not exist for a new tenant, so those modules fail with 'relation does not exist' until someone hand-runs a seed/DDL script.

evidence.snippet: export { departments } ...; export { employees } ...; export { payrollRuns } from './payroll_runs'; export { payslips } ...; export { auditLogs } ...; /* ~24 tables total */
*   PHASE 1 (DDL)  — creates any MISSING tenant tables (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ...)

reproduction: Call createTenantRegistry(company, cr, activity) (the production signup path) then connect to the new tenant_<hex> schema and \dt. Only departments and employees exist. Any payroll/leave/attendance query throws 42P01 relation does not exist.
technicalRisk: Every module beyond org-chart is non-functional on new tenants; runtime errors, failed writes, no audit capture.
businessRisk: A prospect who self-signs-up gets a broken product. Data captured before tables exist is lost. Undermines the multi-tenant onboarding story entirely.
recommendation: Generate tenant DDL from the Drizzle schema (single source of truth) rather than a hand-maintained string. Options: (a) drive drizzle-kit against each new tenant schema via SET search_path; (b) build a per-tenant migration runner that replays an authoritative ordered DDL bundle covering all tables + constraints + indexes + triggers; (c) at minimum, extend generateTenantDDL to the full table set and add a test asserting parity with schema/tenant/index.ts exports.
workaround: Run scripts/seed-rukn-modules.ts (PHASE 1 DDL) against each new tenant manually.
estimatedEffort: L (2-4 days: author authoritative per-tenant DDL/migration mechanism + parity test)

title: Migrations 0001 & 0002 hardcode a single dev tenant schema (tenant_1ed8b6bd3743) — changes never reach other or new tenants
exploitability: Not attacker-driven; deterministic drift affecting all tenants except one hardcoded schema.
description: Migration 0001 (immigration + gcc columns) and 0002 (policy_documents table + indexes) are written against a literal schema name `tenant_1ed8b6bd3743`. They ALTER/CREATE only inside that one developer tenant. Any other existing tenant and every future tenant never receives these columns/tables. Contrast with 0006/0007/0009 which correctly loop over information_schema.schemata LIKE 'tenant\_%'.
expectedBehavior: Schema migrations must apply to every tenant schema (loop over all tenant_<hex> schemas) and be baked into new-tenant provisioning.
actualBehavior: 0001/0002 mutate exactly one schema. Employees.immigration_status / visa_type / iqama_expiry / passport_expiry / occupation_code / skill_level / gcc_status and the policy_documents table exist only in tenant_1ed8b6bd3743.

evidence.snippet: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_1ed8b6bd3743.policy_documents ( ... );

reproduction: grep -roE 'tenant_[a-f0-9]{12}' packages/db/drizzle -> all hits are tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 in 0001 and 0002. Provision a second tenant, query employees.immigration_status -> column does not exist.
technicalRisk: App code that reads immigration_status/visa_type/policy_documents throws for all tenants but one. Silent divergence between canonical Drizzle schema and live tenant schemas.
businessRisk: Right-to-work / iqama-expiry payroll blocking (a core Saudi compliance feature) is inoperative for real tenants; policy document module missing.
recommendation: Rewrite 0001/0002 as DO-loops over all tenant_<hex> schemas (as 0006/0007/0009 do) AND fold the columns/tables into the authoritative provisioning DDL from DB-001. Add a CI check that fails on any literal tenant_<hex> reference in migrations. Also fix the date-vs-text mismatch (see DB-005).
workaround: Manually replay the ALTERs against every existing tenant schema.

title: audit_logs table and its append-only trigger are not part of tenant provisioning — new tenants have no audit trail and no tamper protection
exploitability: An app-role compromise on a new tenant could write/alter sensitive data with zero audit record.
description: generateTenantDDL does not create audit_logs, so new tenants have no audit table at all. Migration 0007 installs the append-only BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger only for schemas that ALREADY contain audit_logs, and runs once at migration time. A tenant created after 0007 was applied therefore gets neither the table nor the trigger. The trigger design itself is correct and idempotent, but it is not wired into provisioning.
expectedBehavior: Every tenant should be created with audit_logs plus the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger so audit entries are immutable from day one.
actualBehavior: New tenants: no audit_logs table (audit writes fail or are skipped). Even if the table were added later by a script, the append-only trigger is not re-installed for tenants created after 0007.

evidence.snippet: generateTenantDDL() returns DDL for departments + employees only; no audit_logs.

-- Only act on schemas that actually have the audit_logs table.
IF EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = s AND table_name = 'audit_logs' ) THEN
export const auditLogs = pgTable('audit_logs', { id, userId, action, entityType, entityId, oldValue, newValue, ipAddress, createdAt });

reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \dt shows no audit_logs. Add the table by hand and note no audit_logs_no_update/no_delete trigger exists -> UPDATE/DELETE on audit rows succeeds.
technicalRisk: No immutable audit trail for sensitive operations on new tenants; tamper protection absent.
businessRisk: Fails PDPL / SEC audit-log requirements; sensitive-operation logging promised in CLAUDE.md security rules is not enforced for real tenants.
recommendation: Add audit_logs (with actor/action/target/before/after/ip/timestamp) and the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger to the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0007 as the backfill for legacy schemas. Add an integration test asserting UPDATE/DELETE on audit_logs raises in a freshly provisioned tenant.
workaround: Run the 0007 DO-block after every tenant creation.

title: iqama unique index and performance indexes are one-time backfills, not part of provisioning — new tenants can store duplicate national IDs and run unindexed
exploitability: Duplicate iqama/national IDs can be inserted on new tenants if the app-layer check is bypassed or races.
description: The partial unique index employees_iqama_unique (0009) and all hot-path indexes (0006) are applied by one-time DO-loops over existing schemas. generateTenantDDL creates employees.iqama_number_enc as plain TEXT with no unique index and none of the perf indexes. New tenants therefore lack DB-level duplicate-iqama protection and lack indexes on notifications, employees, leave_*, documents, payroll, payslips, expenses, final_settlements, compliance_checks, departments, attendance.
expectedBehavior: Provisioning should create the iqama partial unique index and the standard performance indexes for every tenant.
actualBehavior: Only tenants existing when 0006/0009 ran have them; new tenants have neither.

evidence.snippet: EXECUTE format('CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS employees_iqama_unique ON %I.employees (iqama_number_enc) WHERE iqama_number_enc IS NOT NULL;', s);
iqama_number_enc TEXT,   -- created without a unique index
FOR schema_rec IN SELECT schema_name FROM information_schema.schemata WHERE schema_name ~ '^tenant_[a-f0-9]{12}$' LOOP

reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \di shows no employees_iqama_unique and no perf indexes. Insert two employees with the same iqama via raw SQL -> both succeed.
technicalRisk: Duplicate national IDs (payroll/GOSI keyed on iqama); full-table scans degrade as tenant grows.
businessRisk: Duplicate-employee / duplicate-payroll risk; performance regressions for growing tenants.
recommendation: Fold all indexes (incl. the iqama partial unique index) into the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0006/0009 as legacy backfills.
workaround: Replay 0006 and 0009 DO-blocks after each tenant creation.

title: Provisioned employees table is missing immigration/gcc columns the app requires, and migration types (text) diverge from schema (date)
exploitability: Not attacker-driven; runtime failures on immigration/right-to-work features for new tenants.
description: The Drizzle employees schema defines immigration columns (passportExpiry, iqamaExpiry, exitReentryExpiry as `date`; visaType, occupationCode, skillLevel, immigrationStatus, gccStatus). generateTenantDDL omits all of these. Migration 0001 adds them ONLY to tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 (see DB-002) AND declares passport_expiry/iqama_expiry/exit_reentry_expiry as `text`, conflicting with the `date` type in the canonical schema. So new tenants lack the columns; the one hardcoded tenant has them with the wrong type.
expectedBehavior: Provisioned employees table matches employees.ts exactly, including immigration columns with correct `date` types.
actualBehavior: New tenants: columns absent. Hardcoded tenant: columns present but typed text where Drizzle expects date -> type coercion / comparison bugs for expiry logic.

evidence.snippet: passportExpiry: date('passport_expiry'), iqamaExpiry: date('iqama_expiry'), exitReentryExpiry: date('exit_reentry_expiry'), visaType: visaTypeEnum(...), immigrationStatus: immigrationStatusEnum(...).default('valid')
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS passport_expiry text; ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS iqama_expiry text; ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS exit_reentry_expiry text;

reproduction: Provision a new tenant, query employees.iqama_expiry -> column missing. On tenant_1ed8b6bd3743, inspect column type -> text, not date.
technicalRisk: Expiry-window comparisons on text dates are string comparisons; payroll-blocking on expired iqama/passport is unreliable or errors.
businessRisk: Core Saudi right-to-work compliance (payroll block on expired iqama, 90/60/30-day alerts) broken or absent.
recommendation: Include immigration/gcc columns with correct date/enum types in the authoritative provisioning DDL; align 0001 to date types; backfill/convert text->date on the affected schema.
workaround: Manually add columns (as date) to affected tenant schemas.

title: All timestamps use `timestamp` (without time zone) — Riyadh (UTC+3) correctness risk for punch/approval/payroll times
description: The generated migration 0000 contains zero timestamptz columns; every createdAt/updatedAt and event timestamp (punch_in_at, punch_out_at, approved_at, paid_at, completed_at, sent_at, resolved_at, etc.) is `timestamp` without time zone. Serverless writers run in UTC while the business operates in Asia/Riyadh (UTC+3). Naive timestamps store whatever wall clock the writer used with no offset, so a punch recorded in UTC and displayed as Riyadh local (or vice versa) can be off by 3 hours — material for attendance late/early minutes, overtime, and payroll period boundaries. (Pure `date` columns for hire/termination/leave/period_month are acceptable.) Note inconsistency: hand-written migration 0002 uses timestamptz, so the codebase is mixed.
expectedBehavior: Instant-in-time columns should be timestamptz (stored as UTC, rendered in Asia/Riyadh), consistently across schema and migrations.
actualBehavior: timestamp without time zone everywhere in the Drizzle schema; timestamptz only in one ad-hoc migration.

evidence.snippet: "completed_at" timestamp, "created_at" timestamp DEFAULT now() NOT NULL, "updated_at" timestamp DEFAULT now() NOT NULL   -- payroll_runs; grep timestamptz -> 0 matches
punchInAt: timestamp('punch_in_at'), punchOutAt: timestamp('punch_out_at'),
created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now() NOT NULL, updated_at timestamptz DEFAULT now() NOT NULL   -- the only timestamptz use

reproduction: grep -c timestamptz packages/db/drizzle/0000_thin_freak.sql -> 0. Record a punch from a UTC serverless function, render in Riyadh -> 3h skew.
technicalRisk: Off-by-offset in attendance minutes, overtime, and payroll period cutoffs; ambiguous ordering across DST-free but offset boundaries.
businessRisk: Incorrect late/overtime and payroll amounts; disputes; compliance exposure on working-hours records.
recommendation: Migrate instant columns to timestamptz and standardize on UTC storage + Asia/Riyadh presentation. If mass migration is too costly short-term, enforce a single writer timezone (UTC) app-wide and document it; long-term convert to timestamptz.
workaround: Ensure every writer sets time in UTC and every reader converts to Asia/Riyadh explicitly.

title: Field-encryption key falls back to a public hardcoded dev key outside NODE_ENV=production; no key rotation/versioning
exploitability: Any non-production environment (preview/staging) missing FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY encrypts PII under a key committed to the repo — trivially decryptable by anyone with the source.
description: deriveKeys() throws only when NODE_ENV === 'production'. In any other environment (preview, staging, CI, or a misconfigured prod where NODE_ENV isn't exactly 'production'), a missing FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY silently uses DEV_FALLBACK_KEY = 'hrms-dev-only-insecure-field-encryption-key', which is in source control. PII (iqama/passport/IBAN) written in such environments is effectively plaintext. Separately, the construction has no key-versioning/rotation: the marker is a static 'encv1:' and the key label is fixed 'v1'; rotating FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY changes every deterministic ciphertext and makes all previously stored values fail GCM auth on read (decryptField throws), with no dual-key/rewrap path.
expectedBehavior: Missing key should fail closed in every deployed environment (not just production). A key id should be embedded so keys can be rotated with a rewrap/dual-read window.
actualBehavior: Silent dev-key fallback for all non-production NODE_ENV; no rotation mechanism.

evidence.snippet: const MARKER = 'encv1:'; ... const DEV_FALLBACK_KEY = 'hrms-dev-only-insecure-field-encryption-key';

reproduction: Deploy a preview build with NODE_ENV!=='production' and no FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY; write an employee iqama; read the raw column -> ciphertext decryptable with the repo's DEV_FALLBACK_KEY. Rotate the key -> existing rows throw on read.
technicalRisk: PII confidentiality lost in non-prod; no safe key rotation; a bad key rollover bricks reads of all encrypted PII.
businessRisk: PDPL/PII exposure in staging/preview; operational risk if the key is ever rotated or lost.
recommendation: Fail closed whenever the app is deployed (e.g. require the key unless an explicit local/test flag is set), never fall back to a committed key in any hosted environment. Add a key id to the marker (encv1:<keyid>:) and implement dual-read (old+new key) + background rewrap for rotation. Validate presence at startup via packages/config env.
workaround: Set FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY in every deployed environment including preview/staging.
estimatedEffort: M (fail-closed is S; rotation support is M)

title: Single flat Drizzle migration mixes public and all tenant tables into one unqualified schema; no per-tenant migration runner
exploitability: Operator running db:migrate/db:push against the schema-per-tenant DB materializes all tenant tables in public, cross-contaminating the isolation model.
description: drizzle.config.ts scans schema/**/*.ts (both public and tenant). The generated 0000_thin_freak.sql contains 78 CREATE TABLE statements — public tables (accounts, sessions, users, tenants, verification_tokens, invite_token_index) AND all tenant tables (employees, payslips, etc.) — with no schema qualifier and 106 ADD CONSTRAINT FKs, all in a single schema. drizzle-kit migrate/push targets the connection's default schema (public), so running it would create every tenant table in public. There is no per-tenant migration runner; ongoing schema evolution for tenants is done via ad-hoc DO-block or hardcoded migrations (DB-002), which is the root cause of the drift findings above.
expectedBehavior: A clear separation: public-schema migrations for shared tables, and a dedicated per-tenant migration/DDL pipeline replaying an authoritative bundle into each tenant_<hex> schema. drizzle-kit generate should not blend the two.
actualBehavior: One flat migration blends public + tenant tables; no mechanism keeps existing tenant schemas in sync with the Drizzle schema.

evidence.snippet: schema: './src/schema/**/*.ts', out: './drizzle', dialect: 'postgresql', dbCredentials: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL ... }
CREATE TABLE "accounts" ...; CREATE TABLE "employees" ...; CREATE TABLE "payslips" ...; CREATE TABLE "tenants" ...; CREATE TABLE "users" ...   -- public + tenant mixed, unqualified
"db:push": "drizzle-kit push", "db:generate": "drizzle-kit generate", "db:migrate": "drizzle-kit migrate"

reproduction: grep 'CREATE TABLE' 0000_thin_freak.sql -> 78 tables incl. both public and tenant. Run drizzle-kit migrate against the dev DB -> tenant tables land in public.
technicalRisk: Isolation model violated if migrate/push is run; no reproducible way to evolve tenant schemas -> perpetual drift (DB-001..005).
businessRisk: Operational fragility; risk of accidental cross-tenant table creation in public; hard to onboard/maintain tenants at scale.
recommendation: Split schema generation: keep drizzle migrations for public-schema tables only, and build a first-class per-tenant migration runner (ordered SQL bundle applied with search_path per tenant, tracked in a per-tenant migrations table). Regenerate provisioning DDL from the tenant schema. Remove tenant tables from the public-targeted migration path.
workaround: Never run db:migrate/db:push against the shared DB without a schema target; manage tenant DDL via scripts.

title: leave_balances has no uniqueness on (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and no timestamps — duplicate balances / leave double-counting possible
exploitability: A retry/race in balance creation, or a bug, can insert two rows for the same employee/type/year; consumers may sum or pick arbitrarily.
description: leave_balances defines id, employeeId (FK), leaveTypeId (FK), balance numeric(5,1), year integer — with no unique constraint across (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and no created_at/updated_at. Nothing at the DB level prevents duplicate balance rows for the same key, and there is no audit of when a balance changed.
expectedBehavior: Exactly one balance row per employee per leave type per year, enforced by a unique constraint, with change timestamps.
actualBehavior: Duplicates are permitted; no temporal audit columns.

evidence.snippet: export const leaveBalances = pgTable('leave_balances', { id, employeeId, leaveTypeId, balance: numeric('balance',{precision:5,scale:1}).notNull(), year: integer('year').notNull() });  // no unique(), no timestamps
CREATE TABLE "leave_balances" ( "id" ..., "employee_id" uuid NOT NULL, "leave_type_id" uuid NOT NULL, "balance" numeric(5,1) NOT NULL, "year" integer NOT NULL );

reproduction: Insert two rows with identical employee_id, leave_type_id, year -> both succeed.
technicalRisk: Ambiguous/duplicated balances; consumers may double-count or read the wrong row.
recommendation: Add UNIQUE (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and created_at/updated_at columns; dedupe existing data before applying. Include in provisioning DDL (DB-001).
workaround: Enforce single-row invariant in application upsert logic (ON CONFLICT once the unique index exists).

title: PII '_enc' columns are plaintext TEXT at the DB level — encryption is enforced only by the app-layer Drizzle codec
exploitability: Any write path that bypasses the Drizzle encryptedText codec (raw SQL, seed scripts, psql, db.execute, bulk import) stores plaintext PII in a column whose name implies it is encrypted.
description: iqama_number_enc / passport_number_enc / bank_iban_enc are ordinary TEXT columns; confidentiality depends entirely on every writer going through the encryptedText customType. The codec even passes through non-marked (legacy plaintext) values on read, so a plaintext write is invisible on read and silently defeats encryption-at-rest. Seed scripts and any db.execute raw path are realistic bypasses.
expectedBehavior: PII-at-rest guarantee should not depend on developer discipline; ideally a DB-level guard (e.g., a CHECK/trigger requiring the encv1: marker) or centralized write path.
actualBehavior: Plaintext can land in _enc columns with no error and no read-time signal.

evidence.snippet:
iqama_number_enc TEXT,
      passport_number_enc TEXT,
      bank_iban_enc TEXT,
export function decryptField(stored: string): string { if (!stored.startsWith(MARKER)) return stored; // legacy plaintext passes through

reproduction: INSERT into employees (iqama_number_enc) VALUES ('1234567890') via raw SQL; row reads back as plaintext with isEncrypted=false and no error.
technicalRisk: Silent plaintext PII at rest via any non-codec writer.
businessRisk: PDPL/PII exposure that passes unnoticed; audits may wrongly assume all _enc data is encrypted.
recommendation: Add a DB CHECK/trigger enforcing the encv1: marker on _enc columns (allowing a time-boxed legacy window), or route all writes through a single repository. Add a lint/test that fails if raw writes target _enc columns. Complete the encrypt-pii-backfill and then tighten the constraint.
workaround: Code review to ensure only Drizzle codec writes these columns; run backfill.

title: users.mfa_secret (TOTP seed) is stored plaintext, not wrapped in encryptedText
exploitability: A read of the users table (SQLi, backup leak, DB access) yields TOTP seeds, letting an attacker generate valid second factors and defeat MFA.
description: The users table stores mfaSecret as plain text. Unlike employee iqama/passport/IBAN, the MFA shared secret is not wrapped in the encryptedText codec, so the TOTP seed sits at rest in cleartext. Anyone able to read the row can reconstruct the user's one-time codes.
expectedBehavior: MFA/TOTP secrets should be encrypted at rest (or stored in a dedicated secret store), consistent with the SEC-008 encryption applied to other PII.

evidence.snippet: mfaSecret: text('mfa_secret'),
CREATE TABLE "users" ( ... "mfa_secret" text, ... )   -- no encryption

reproduction: Read users.mfa_secret -> base32 TOTP seed in cleartext; feed to any TOTP generator to produce valid codes.
technicalRisk: MFA bypass if the users table is exposed.
businessRisk: Account takeover risk; weakens the MFA control the product advertises.
recommendation: Wrap mfa_secret with the encryptedText codec (or a KMS-backed secret store). Since MFA secrets need no equality/uniqueness, prefer a randomized-IV variant rather than the deterministic codec to avoid equality leakage. Backfill existing rows.
workaround: Restrict DB read access; rotate MFA enrollments if exposure suspected.

title: Deterministic (SIV) field encryption leaks plaintext equality — deliberate, documented tradeoff for the iqama unique index
exploitability: An observer of ciphertext can tell which rows share the same plaintext (e.g., two employees with the same IBAN) but cannot recover the plaintext.
description: encryptField derives the AES-GCM IV as HMAC(ivKey, plaintext) truncated to 12 bytes (a synthetic-IV/SIV construction), making ciphertext deterministic. This is required so the iqama partial unique index and equality lookups work on the encrypted column. The known cost is that equal plaintexts produce equal ciphertexts (equality is observable). The code is a correct authenticated-encryption construction: AES-256-GCM, 16-byte tag, domain-separated enc/iv subkeys via HMAC labels, tamper rejection on read (verified by crypto.test.ts). Recording as a strength with a documented caveat, not a defect.
expectedBehavior: For a column needing a unique index, deterministic encryption is the accepted design; equality leakage is inherent and acknowledged in code comments.
actualBehavior: As designed. Note: fields that do NOT need equality/uniqueness (e.g., bank_iban_enc, and mfa_secret per DB-011) would be better served by randomized IVs to avoid unnecessary equality leakage.

evidence.snippet: // The IV is derived from the plaintext ... making the ciphertext DETERMINISTIC ... The trade-off is that equality of plaintexts is observable ... acceptable and necessary here.

reproduction: encryptField('X') === encryptField('X') (true, by design); different plaintexts differ.
technicalRisk: Equality-pattern leakage on encrypted columns; low sensitivity for identifiers already unique.
businessRisk: Minimal; frequency analysis only reveals duplicate values.
recommendation: Keep deterministic encryption for iqama (unique-index requirement). For fields not needing equality (IBAN, MFA secret), consider a randomized-IV variant to remove equality leakage. Document the field-by-field choice.
